coredata.dc:title: In situ simultaneous chemical activation and exfoliation of carbon quantum dots for atmospheric adsorption of H2S and CO2 at room temperature

coredata.dc:description: Herein, novel nitrogen-doped nanoporous carbons possessing 2.63 at.% N and high porosity were prepared using nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) via a facile chemical activation. To this end, after performing the hydrothermal process on the raw materials, chemical activation was carried out by various KOH/N-CQDs ratios (2:1 to –4:1), resulting in unique micro-mesoporous structures (i.e., HNAC samples). In this approach, a large surface area (1477 m2g−1) and pore volume (1.05 cm3g−1) were obtained, where the micropore volume was increased by decreasing the KOH/N-CQDs ratio, activation time and augmenting the activation temperature, that caused considerable adsorption at 1 bar. It is worth mentioning that mesopores could be generated by condition optimization that led to higher adsorption at high pressure. Due to their large surface area and high nitrogen loading, the HNAC samples demonstrated excellent H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity at ambient conditions (9.28 mmolg−1 for H2S and 4.28 mmolg−1 for CO2 at 1 bar and 25 °C) and at higher pressures (18.0 mmolg−1 for H2S and 6.20 mmolg−1 for CO2 at 10 bar and 25 °C). According to the significant performance of the prepared nanosorbents along with stable cyclic adsorption-desorption performance, the HNACs are proposed as promising adsorbents with outstanding adsorption.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, novel nitrogen-doped nanoporous carbons possessing 2.63 at.% N and high porosity were prepared using nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) via a facile chemical activation. To this end, after performing the hydrothermal process on the raw materials, chemical activation was carried out by various KOH/N-CQDs ratios (2:1 to –4:1), resulting in unique micro-mesoporous structures (i.e., HNAC samples). In this approach, a large surface area (1477 m2g−1) and pore volume (1.05 cm3g−1) were obtained, where the micropore volume was increased by decreasing the KOH/N-CQDs ratio, activation time and augmenting the activation temperature, that caused considerable adsorption at 1 bar. It is worth mentioning that mesopores could be generated by condition optimization that led to higher adsorption at high pressure. Due to their large surface area and high nitrogen loading, the HNAC samples demonstrated excellent H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity at ambient conditions (9.28 mmolg−1 for H2S and 4.28 mmolg−1 for CO2 at 1 bar and 25 °C) and at higher pressures (18.0 mmolg−1 for H2S and 6.20 mmolg−1 for CO2 at 10 bar and 25 °C). According to the significant performance of the prepared nanosorbents along with stable cyclic adsorption-desorption performance, the HNACs are proposed as promising adsorbents with outstanding adsorption.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its cleaner and better energy efficiency, natural gas has attracted much attention compared to using petroleum. Natural gas treatment is a key step in its usage where pollutants such as carbon dioxide (CO2) and sulfur compounds (H2S as the main component) need to be removed [1]. Diverse methods of separation and purification, including the chemical absorption by amines [2], physical adsorption by bulk adsorbent [3], membrane separation [4], and catalytic oxidation [5] have been developed and proposed. In this respect, based on the chemical absorption, extensive technologies and works have been developed by alkanolamines and also by employing nanosorbents [6–9]. However, due to the chemisorption effects and high regeneration costs associated with porous sorbents employed in removing H2S, a few applicable adsorbents have been exploited for this purpose. Therefore, extensive research works have been devoted to designing and synthesizing adsorbents to mitigate the current severe issues. For example, we utilized the MIL-101(Cr) metal organic framework (MOF) to investigate H2S and CO2 gas uptake [10,11] and catalytic oxidation of H2S [12]. Because of their low price and environmentally friendly nature, carbon nanomaterials have been introduced as promising candidates in this regard.

Owing to their unique physicochemical properties, carbon quantum dots (CQDs, with a diameter below 10 nm [13]) have been employed in different applications such as bio-imaging [14], sensing [15,16], catalysis [17–19], optoelectronic conversion [20,21], energy storage [22] and nanomedicine [23]. Two different approaches, including surface functionalization and heteroatom doping, have been used to improve the physicochemical performance and expand applications of CQDs. Complex synthesis, purification steps with low production yield, and occupying the position functionalized for specific analytical are the drawbacks of surface functionalization [24]. On the other hand, heteroatom doping, which is relatively convenient for large-scale synthesis, has been reported as a more powerful approach to improve CQDs properties [25–27]. In CQDs, the high nitrogen content can be readily obtained by using proper nitrogen-containing precursors. Thus, one expects to exploit the potential application of N-CQDs for preparation of high nitrogen-doping activated carbon by the chemical activation.

As is well known, the initial precursor and also the synthesis conditions have significant and determining influences on the final chemical and textural properties of activated carbon (ACs). Moreover, functionalizing the ACs can effectively improve the adsorptive capability through intensifying the interactions among the carbon nanosorbents and the adsorbates [28]. To synthesize the ACs, generally, the precursor materials which are rich in carbon are first carbonized, after which, an activating process occurs through the chemical or physical activation process [29]. In this regard, it has been reported that chemical activation by employing the KOH is almost the most suitable route to induce micropores and also promote the specific surface area for a better adsorption performance. The ACs are applicable for the adsorption of many gaseous compounds. The adsorption capacity of AC decreases while augmenting the temperature; therefore, the ACs are well suited for applications occurring at low temperatures, especially for H2S and CO2 uptake. Currently, several studies have been devoted to developing effective and high-performing ACs with high adsorption capacity, good reproducibility, and thermal durability for H2S and CO2 adsorptive removal processes, particularly at low temperatures [30,31]. Jacobs et al. [32] focused on preparing the AC from the petcoke precursor, which was done through chemical activation and resulted in a surface area of 2671 m2 g−1 and displayed both a high-capacity and selectivity towards H2S and SO2. Volumetric adsorption measurements on SO2, H2S, CO2, CH4, N2, and O2 showed that the prepared AC adsorbed 39 and 19 times more SO2 and H2S than N2, respectively. Mochizuki et al. [33] prepared AC from the petcoke and examined properties, including the pore structure, surface chemistry, and also the H2S adsorption capacity. In addition to advantages created by tuning the porosity of the porous sorbents, as reported by nitrogen doping such structures, the adsorbent-adsorbate interaction intensity can be enhanced considerably. Tehrani et al. [8] prepared a highly porous N-S-rich nanoporous carbon from asphaltene residue with a high surface area of (2186 m2 g−1) and pore volume of (1.3 cm3 g−1) with a considerable performance in the H2S and CO2 uptake. Feng et al. [34] found a linear relationship between the H2S adsorption capacity of the sorbents and the amount of the basic functionalities, highlighting the role of surface chemistry in further improving the adsorption capacity. Based on previous reports, the maximum adsorption capacity of ACs in hydrogen sulfide removal is almost 358 mg g−1 (Table 3) [35–40]. This has been ackmplished via modifying the surface properties (augmenting the specific surface area and optimizing the pore size distribution) and surface functionalization, especiall by creating basic groups. Due to the recent interest in establishing novel methods to synthesize carbon-based materials, hydrothermal treatment (HT) has been introduced as an effective approach to obtain N-CQDs. The HT has been chosen instead of traditional carbonization methods, as it requires milder conditions [41]. Herein, exploiting N-CQDs is reported as an efficient method to adjust the pore size distribution and also functional groups of the activated carbons.

In the present research, first, an innovative strategy is proposed and demonstrated by synthesizing nitrogen-rich CQDs and KOH chemical activation for the preparation of activated carbon with high nitrogen-doping (HNAC). Morphological, textural, and chemical characterizations of N-CQDs and HNACs were investigated. The developed carbon structures were used as H2S and CO2 absorbents, where they demonstrated outstanding performance at low and high pressure. The recorded adsorption isotherms were also fitted on three mathematical models, including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir-Freundlich models. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report in the literature regarding the preparation of the HNAC from N-CQDs and its use in equilibrium gas adsorption. As N-CQDs is synthesized using several precursors such as waste, which contains nitrogen-rich functional groups, the present work provides detailed information on the HNAC, which can serve as a model material.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (CH4N2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 1 mol L-1) were purchased from Merck and Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). DI water was used in all experiments. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without any further purification.

N-doped CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal method proposed by Qu et al. [42] with some modifications. According to this method, 4.2 g (20 mmol) citric acid and 3.6 g (60 mmol) urea were solved in 100 mL DI water and stirred for 30 min, from which a clear solution was obtained. In the next step, the prepared solution was placed in a 200 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave which was then heated to 160 °C for almost 6 h in an electric oven. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the final products were centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 20 min and filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to separate them from agglomerates and larger particles. The N-CQDs were obtained by drying the final product at 80 °C for 24 h. The solid can be easily re-dispersed in water.

The N-CQDs were activated in order to create new pores and also augment the specific surface area. In a typical synthesis procedure, N-CQDs (3.4 g) were physically mixed and homogenized with KOH powder at different mass ratios of 2–4, using a mortar and pestle. The mixture was heat-treated in a stainless steel reactor, which was placed inside a muffle furnace under N2 flow (200 mL min−1). During the process, the temperature was first raised to 200 °C with a heating rate of 25 °C min−1, and held at this temperature for 1 h. Thereinafter, the activation step was carried out at 600–900 °C with the heating rate of 10 °C min−1, then the mixture was activated at high temperature for 0–2 h. After cooling down the samples to room temperature under nitrogen flow, the black solid residue was washed with HCl (1 mol L-1) to remove inorganic compounds, followed by hot and cold DI water until the pH value reached neutral. The prepared carbon products dried under 110 °C for 24 h and stored in an airtight container for further experiments. The synthesized products were labeled as HNAC-xyz, where ×, y, and z represent the temperature, time, and KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio, respectively. For instance, HNAC-812 stands for the sample which has been activated at 800 °C, for 1 h with a KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio of 2.

According to our previous works [10,11], to evaluate the equilibrium adsorption capacity of H2S and CO2 on the newly developed nanocarbonaceous HNAC adsorbents, a volumetric setup was exploited. The schematic illustration of the employed setup is represented in Fig. 1 . The adsorption experiments and also the isotherms were recorded in the pressure range of 1–10 bar and varied temperatures, according to the conditions employed in industrials units. After weighting the adsorbent and loading it into the adsorption column, all lines were totally sealed. Prior to performing the adsorption experiments, the lines were vacuumed so that to evacuate the remaining unfavorable gases (i.e., H2S, CO2 or He). Afterwards, degassing of the samples was done at 120 °C for 4 h under vacuum conditions to desorb the adsorbates such as moisture, air, and CO2. The regeneration process was done by adjusting temperature and pressure through employing a heating jacket and vacuum pump. Furthermore, the process temperature was maintained at 25 °C via utilizing a circulator system. To degas during the experiments, V-1 to V-5 valves were completely closed, however, the V-6 and V-7 valves were fully open. In the adsorption process, vent streams were closed by V-4 and V-7 valves and the specified gas filled the holder tank (i.e., H2S and CO2) at a given pressure, called the initial pressure (

). In the following, the V-6 valve is opened while the V-5 valve is kept closed, and the holder tank pressure is decreased to reach the equilibrium pressure of (

). The decrease in the gas pressure originates from the dead volume of the lines and rig and also over the sorbent. Eventually, to deduce the dead volume of the adsorption setup, He uptake was measured at 10 bar.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Philips, PW-1730) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in 2θ = 10–80 and step size of 0.05 was employed to assess phase purity, crystallinity and structure of the prepared HNACs samples. The scanning rate was three degrees min−1 and the operating power was 40 kV and 40 mA. The surface functional groups were studied by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Jasco, Japan) from 500 to 4000 cm−1 with KBr Pellets. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Tescan, Mira II instrument) was used to observe the morphology and particle size of the samples. SEM/Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy analysis was carried out for determining the elemental compositions before and after the adsorption experiments. The morphology and size of particles were also examined with high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G20, USA) operating at 200 kV. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were obtained on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique. Surface Analysis with Al Kα X-ray monochromator, pass energy 20 eV. Textural characterization was carried out by measuring the N2 adsorption-desorption at 77 K on a micrometrics apparatus (BELSORP MINI II). Combined with the Brunauer, Emmett and Teller (BET) method, isotherms were used to calculate the specific surface area and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method was employed for calculating total pore volume, pore size distributions and average pore width. Prior to nitrogen adsorption-desorption experiment, the samples were degassed at 200 °C for 3 h to remove undesired molecules and moisture from micro-mesopores.

The XRD patterns of the prepared N-CQDs and HNAC samples are shown in Fig. 2 a. The characteristic peak of graphite at 2θ = 27.5° correspond to the (002) plane which appeared in the XRD pattern of N-CQDs. The diffraction patterns for HANC-812 and 802 displayed two broad and low-intensity peaks at around 2θ = 24° and 43°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of carbon in the graphitic structure, indicating that the HANCs are of an amorphous nanocarbon structure. It can be seen that for HNAC-824, the diffraction peak related to (100) vanished and (002) became more intense and shifted to 2θ = 27°. This demonstrated that as the mass ratio of KOH/N-CQDs increased, the structure shifted toward the formation of graphite crystallites. The increment of the background signal in the diffraction degree range of 2θ = 10-20° can be attributed to the formation of micropores during the activation process. Actually, the created micropores can scatter the diffracted X-ray beams, accordingly, raise the background signal [43]. The interlayer spacing (d002) of HNAC nanosorbent can be determined with Bragg’s equation, as shown below:
d 002
=
λ
2 sin θ
Where λ is the X-ray wavelength (λ = 0.154 nm) and θ is the peak position.

The interlayer spacing, d002, values for both the HANC-812 and 802 were found to be 0.370 nm which is larger than that of the graphite (d002 = 0.335 nm); whereas, the d002 value for N-CQDs and HNAC-824 were 0.324 and 0.330 nm, respectively. The higher interlayer spacing (d002) can be ascribed to disordered nature of HNAC samples where a proper graphitization did not take place [44]. This structure results in higher porosity and surface area which is beneficial for great adsorption capability.

FTIR was employed to characterize the functional groups of the synthesized N-CQDs and HNAC samples (Fig. 2b and 2c). As can be seen in Fig. 2b, the broad absorption peak observed in the 3100–3400 cm−1 range is related to the presence of hydroxyl groups. Besides, the N–H stretching vibration peak was also overlapping in this region. The absorption characteristic of C–O, C–N, and C–H appeared at 1167, 1318 and 1400 cm−1, respectively. The peaks observed at 1569 cm−1 and 764 cm−1 resulted from the stretching vibration of aromatic C = C and C–H bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of the graphene basic component. According to Fig. 2c, the synthesized HNAC nanoadsorbants exhibited the intense and broad band at around 3420 cm−1 which is attributed to O–H stretching vibration and/or N–H symmetric stretching vibration [45]. Regarding the spectral assignments, two bands at 2917 and 2848 cm−1 were respectively due to presence of asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibration of CH2–NH–CH3 or CH2–NH–CH2– groups [46,47]. The weak vibration band at 1698 cm−1 was identified as C = O stretching mode of nonaromatic carboxylic groups [46], while the peak appearing at around 1050 cm−1 is related to the C–O and C–N stretching vibration [48] and we can see higher intensity in this band as the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio increased, which is due to the more carbon atoms consumption and is compatible with the EDS results. The characteristic peak at around 1630 cm−1 is alluded to the C = C vibration in aromatic rings, an index approving that prepared nanoporous carbons structure comprised aromatic rings [8] and also assigned to pyrrole and C = N presence [49]. A group of bands could be observed between 500 and 800 cm−1, which can be related to C–H and C = C stretching vibrations in aromatic structures [28]. Therefore, the FTIR analysis confirms that nitrogen functional groups were introduced successfully.

The morphology and microstructure of the novel synthesized HNAC samples were examined by FE-SEM. Depending on the activation times, activation temperatures and mass ratios, the external surface of the HNACs had pores with different sizes and shapes. Fig. 3 a and 3b illustrates that the HNAC-812 had a high porosity with a relatively narrow size distribution and the particles were clustered in uniform nanosheet fragments. Fig. 3c and 3d demonstrates different sizes of pores on HNAC-802 sample. The particles were irregularly shaped with the swollen surface. It seems that nanosheets did not have enough time to exfoliate properly. These results indicate that the amorphous and spherical structure of N-CQDs was destroyed and graphene-like structures with a dense porosity feature was formed through the KOH activation. The structure of HNAC-824 (Fig. 3e and 3f) contained small broken pieces of amorphous particles with scaly-like surface which shows that a few scattered pores were generated on the surface. Despite the behavior seen in the literature [8], increasing the amount of KOH from HNAC-812 to HNAC-824 significantly reduced the pore volume of the carbon microstructure and eliminated a considerable amount of defects. Additional FE-SEM images of the HNAC samples are provided in Fig. S1.

As may be seen from Fig. S2, the peak of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen appeared in the EDS graphs of the fresh N-CQDs raw material, HNAC-812 and HNAC-824 surface, while the peak of sulfur observed on HNAC-812 surface after the adsorption of H2S and before regeneration. EDS can detect up to 1 μm of the sample depth, and the most of the particles especially nanosheets are under 1 μm in thickness; thus, the surface characterization would be expected to be representative of the entire material. The amount of each of these elements is listed in Table S1.

In order to clarify the microstructure and morphology of HNAC nanocarbon based adsorbents, HRTEM method was employed (Fig. 4 ). The TEM images in Fig. 4a and 4c confirms that the HNAC-812 and 802 samples were made of highly porous particles with a 2D morphology. The HRTEM images with 200 nm scale (Fig. 4b and 4d) clearly shows the uniform and ordered holes with diameters of about 50 nm. Therefore, the possible mechanism of the synthesis process can be considered as follows: CQDs demonstrated an amorphous nature with a spherical morphology consisting of very small interconnected graphitic layers (size of primarily used CQDs were found to be less than 10 nm according to photoluminescence (PL) spectra and TEM image as seen in Figs. S3 and S4). During the KOH activation process, penetration of metallic potassium into the graphitic layers of carbon-core in CQDs at high temperature, first caused disruption and separation of the carbon layers and then the edges of the individual layers were bonded together. As a result, larger graphene sheets were formed with an excellent porosity and surface area. The sharpness of the HNAC-812 and 802 images indicates that few layer graphene-like nanosheets were stacked and the cavities were clearly recognizable. However, the HNAC-824 sample consisted mostly of carbon particles which tended to agglomerate and practically no significant micropores are observed (Fig. 4e and 4f). The HRTEM microphotographs are illustrated in inset Fig. 4b, 4d and 4f, which were taken on the edge of HNAC nanosheets, clearly reveal that the porosity was made of randomly oriented narrow micropores and confirm the presence of intermittent graphitic layers that are useful in the adsorption process. Thereby, the above interpretations are in line with the results obtained from the nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements. Additional HRTEM images of the HNAC samples are provided in Fig. S5. As clearly seen in Fig. 4g-i, comparing the elemental mapping (carbon and nitrogen) revealed that HNAC-812 and 802 samples possessed the highest carbon and nitrogen content, with a favorable elemental distribution compared to the HNAC-824 sample, which can significantly improve the adsorption capability, especially at low partial pressures.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the prepared carbonaceous nanoadosrbents, are illustrated in Fig. 5 , from which valuable information regarding the porosity nature of the samples can be achieved. Based on the IUPAC classification, N-CQDs showed an isotherm with features of a low porous material, while N2 isotherms of synthesized HNAC-612, 712, 812 and 802 samples with the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio of 2, temperature and activation time of 600–800 °C and 0–1 h, respectively, can be categorized into Type-I profiles; this is attributed to the formation of a porous structure comprising micro-mesopores [50]. Typically, the sharp initial nitrogen uptake in a low range of relative pressure (P/P0 < 0.01) can be explained by the presence of micropores in the carbon structure. On the other hand, the significant H4 hysteresis loop at a relative pressure of higher than 0.45 is ascribed to the occurrence of mesopores within the carbon structure with a capillary condensation, which also represents narrow slit-like pores (Fig. 5a).

However, as seen in Fig. 5b, with an increase in the mass ratio of KOH/N-CQDs to 3 and 4, temperature and activation time to 900 °C and 2 h, the shape of N2 isotherms of the synthesized nanosorbents (HNAC-822, 813, 823, 814, 824 and 912) seems to be nearly type IV isotherms. As it was detected, in a low pressure range of the isotherm, a steep increase in the adsorption isotherm occurred along with raising the relative pressure. It can be concluded that the prepared nanocarbonaceous samples were composed of a certain amounts of micropores. Moreover, in the P/P0 range of 0.1 and 0.45, the isotherm behavior followed the type II which is originated from the occurrence of the monoloayer adsorption followed by a multilayer adsorption [29]. Finally, at higher P/P0 values, in the range of 0.45 and 1.0, the type H3 hysteresis loop was emerged which is attributed to domination of mesopores within the carbon network.

The textural parameters of N-CQDs and all synthesized HNAC samples are listed in Table 1 . As can be observed, N-CQDs practically did not have pores and the use of KOH as an activating agent significantly increased the micropore content and promoted the development of mesopores on the HNAC surface. Contrary to other reports related to activated carbon, the increasing trend of KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio from 2 to 4, decreased the surface area (1477–114 m2 g−1), total pore volume (1.05–0.45 cm3 g−1), micropore volume (0.92–0.13 cm3 g−1) and Vmicro / Vtotal (0.88–0.28); however, the average pore width increased from 3.06 to 15.61 nm (e.g. HNAC-81z). To be specific, based on the 6KOH + 2C → 2 K + 3H2 + 2K2CO3 reaction occurring at the activating temperature, the further utilization of KOH not only consumes more carbon and destroys the wall of the micropores, but also generates enlarged pores and shifts the sample toward a mesoporous structure and thus the surface area decreases [29,51]. Despite the fact that most of the reported literature use a KOH/precursor mass ratio of 4 for the preparation of activated carbon, HNAC-xy2 nanoadsorbents is the most cost-effective adsorbent. It is clear that as the temperature of activation rose from 600 to 800 °C, the specific surface area (1120–1477 m2 g−1), total pore volume (0.75–1.05 cm3 g−1) and micropores volume (0.60–0.92 cm3 g−1) were steadily increased. It is ascribed to the fact that an increase in the temperature results in raising the energy transferred to the carbon atoms during the activation process, with more carbon atoms consumed on active sites, and accordingly the surface area and pore volume will be further improved. At the temperature of 800 °C that elemental potassium exists, the potassium vapor can be readily diffused into the initial pores and graphite layers which significantly increases the pores volume. The further increase in the temperature to more than 800 °C, increases the K-ion diffusion rate, which in turn accelerates the carbon reaction rate. Therefore, a portion of the micropores is sintered, which reduces the specific surface area of the nanoporous carbons. In addition, along with incrementing the temperature, the pores will be ablated excessively, leading to raising the average pore width by 17.37 nm and also to considerable reduction of the specific surface area. To investigate the influence of activation time, it can be seen that with an increase in the activation time to 1 h, the specific surface area (1544–1477 m2 g−1) decreased slightly, while total pore volume (0.95–1.05 cm3 g−1) and micropores volume (0.80–0.92 cm3 g−1) increased gradually compared to the initial stage. Then, the specific surface area, total and micropore volume drastically decreased with augmenting the activating time to 2 h. At the initial stages of the activation process, when the activation duration was augmented, the reaction between carbons and the KOH agent was ameliorated and new pores were created. When the activation time reached 1 h, the activating reaction reached a saturated stage, and the pore volume reached the maximum value. After that, the carbon skeleton atoms are consumed and the original micropores are converted to mesopores. The surface characteristics of the HNAC nanocarbonaceous samples are comparable with those reported in the literature (Table S2).

The pore size distribution curves related to the HNAC samples are demonstrated in Fig. 5c and 5d. According to Fig. 5c, the pore size distributions of the samples are similar and the pores size of the nanoporous carbons was lower than 8 nm; this implies that both micro and mesopores were created. In addition, there were intensive peaks at a radius of 1.2 nm located in the narrow mesopores area (2 ≤ D ≤ 50 nm) and the peak intensity rose with an increase in the activation temperature. The HNAC-812 and 802 are dominantly microporous, as they contained a high micropore volume, actually greated than 80% of the total pore volume; as declared in the literature, the micropores are well suited for gas phase desulfurization and CO2 removal [52]. In Fig. 5d, HNAC-813 and 823 show a shift in the pore size distributions toward increasing the size of the formed pores. However, the pore size distributions confirm that two pore systems were induced in the nanoporous carbons. The main maximum peak was at a radius of 1.2 nm, and the latter was enlarged to about 25 nm. Both the HNAC-814 and 824 exhibited an extensive pore size distribution and were mainly composed of large mesopores with an average pore width of 15.61 and 23.05 nm, respectively.

The chemical structure of synthesized samples was further studied by XPS. This analysis was performed on HNAC-802, 812 and 824 samples as representatives of HNAC nanoadsorbents with the highest and lowest gas uptake. The XPS survey spectra confirm the existence of three major peaks at 284.8, 400.37 and 532.33 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, as shown in Fig. S6 and Table 2
.

The high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 6 a) can be fitted to four different peaks assigned to C–C/C = C, C–N, C–O, and C = N/C = O. Additionally, the high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 6b) was fitted by two Gaussian peaks which suggests that two different nitrogen containining species were formed atop the sample. The Peaks centered in the range of 399.1–399.98 and 401.3–402.38 eV can be assigned to pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively [26,53]. Besides the high level of nitrogen doping obtained in the nanoporous carbons, the half of nitrogen species content on the HNACs surface were allocated to the graphitic-N species, which is reported to be stable and possesses a better acid gas adsorption capabilty [54]. In Fig. 6c, the O1s deconvolution demonstrated that a significant portion of the surface was composed of various oxygen species. Based on published reports[68,56], the resolved peaks under O1s spectra were attributed to three primary functional groups: C = O, C-O-H and C-O-C. These findings are additionally confirmed by the FTIR spectra.

The data shown in Table 2 suggest that the intensities of the C1s, N1s and O1s peaks for HNAC-802 and 812 were close to each other. Therefore, the activation time interval of 0–1 h does not have a significant effect on the chemical structure of these adsorbents. However, as the mass ratio of KOH/N-CQDs increased, the intensities of the C1s peaks, especially C–C/C = C, decreased, while the relative area of the peak assigned to C-O on O1s spectra increased. This indicates that during the reaction of carbon with excessive activating agent, some C-C/C = C bonds on carbon skeleton were broken and some C-O groups were formed on the surface of the HNAC-824 adsorbent, resulting in the ablation of micropores. These observations correspond to the BET results, showing a decrease in the number of micropores and an increase in the pore size for HNAC-824 nanoadsorbent. On the other hand, increasing the mass ratio of KOH/N-CQDs reduced the total number of N, which could be a source of basic centers. The N1s XPS spectra proved that the N doping amount in HNAC-812 was boosted to 2.63 at%, which is much higher than 0.94 at% for HNAC-824. Furthermore, more pyrrolic-N was converted to the more stable graphitic-N species when the activation time increased.

Notably, the elemental analysis of N-CQDs showed the high nitrogen content (16.49 at.%) which makes it a desired precursor for the production of nanoporous carbon. This level of nitrogen functional groups, embedded in the precursor, can have a significant influence on the gas adsorption capacity of the synthesized samples. In addition, the hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of N-CQDs were measured by means of the DLS test. As shown in Fig. S7a, the average size distribution of N-CQDs was recorded to be below the 10 nm. The average zeta potential value for N-CQDs was –22.8 ± 7.7 mV. The zeta potential analysis shows that the as-synthesized N-CQDs were negatively charged, suggesting that the N-CQDs contained oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface (Fig. S7b).

Based on the experimental data above, a possible formation mechanism of the HNACs is demonstrated in Fig. 7 . At 200 °C heating, N-CQDs cross-linking occurred, which prevented their collapsing and maintained the mesospaces between N-CQDs. Then, the cross-linked N-CQDs were activated at high temperature and formed a porous nitrogen-rich carbon framework. Due to the porous structural features, a high degree of nitrogen-doping (2.63 at%), a high specific surface area (1477 m2 g−1), and abundant micro/mesopores, rapid diffusion and high adsorption of gas molecules in the HNACs are expected, which are primarily important for adsorbents.

The formation of nitrogen-rich activated carbon is related to the heat-treatment of N-CQDs with KOH under an inert atmosphere. To remove the inorganic impurities (i.e., potassium compounds), the carbon based materials can be washed by a diluted HCl solution. The chemical reactions and K intercalation during the heat-treatment can affect the carbon microstructure and develop micropores within the network. Firstly, KOH reacts with elemental carbons to form potassium carbonate under 650 °C and then is decomposed to K2O at higher temperatures (reaction (7)). Thus, microporosities are generated through reaction (8) of the produced CO2 with carbon materials. After reaching the K boiling point at about 800 °C, K2O is reduced by the carbon atoms to yield a metallic potassium (reaction (9)). This potassium plays a definitive role in generating more porosity because its vapors would diffuse into the graphene layers, resulting in the swelling and destruction of the carbon microstructure. The reactions are as follows [57]:
K2CO3 CO2 + K2O
CO2 + C 2CO
K2O + C 2 K + CO

The weight loss steps in different temperature ranges can be attributed to the destruction of the organic moiety and the gasification process, respectively. The results demonstrated that employing the N-CQDs as carbon source enables the preparation of novel adsorbents in a single step by integrating the carbonization and activation processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2S and CO2 adsorption isotherms on HNAC nanoadsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The H2S and CO2 equilibrium adsorption isotherms on raw N-CQDs and HNAC series, at 1–10 bar and 25 °C, are represented in Fig. 8 a and 8b. The large theoretical specific surface area and abundant defects and edges enabled N-CQDs to be effective nanoadsorbants for gas uptake. However, it was observed that N-CQDs tend to aggregate during the thermal drying process, which results in a substantial loss of active surface area and thus the adsorption capacity of H2S and CO2 gases might be neglected.

As can be seen, most HNAC nanoadsorbents displayed the type-I isotherms for both adsorbate molecules, i.e. H2S and CO2. Nevertheless, the H2S adsorption on the prepared samples was better than that for the CO2, over the whole investigated pressure range (1–10 bar), in which, with steep slopes correspond to the strong interactions between H2S molecules and HNAC surface, with a higher intensity compared to CO2 ones. Considering the regular pore size distribution of HNACs, the high polarizability of H2S (38.0*10-25 cm3) versus CO2 (26.3*10-25 cm3) molecule [11,58] was the central reading leading to the high electrostatic interaction with the adsorbents surface and functional groups of the nanoadsorbent and, consequently, the sharp gas adsorption, especially at low pressures (P ≤ 2 bar). Moreover, the nitrogen-containing functional groups which are basic in nature, establish acid-base interactions with the acidic H2S and CO2 gases and these molecules can also form strong hydrogen bonds with nitrogen-containing functional groups; both these interactions are stronger with H2S, leading to the better adsorption capacity at room temperature. On the other side, H2S and CO2 molecules can be adsorbed on the active sites of the samples’ surface through the weak van der Waals bondings. The gas adsorption behavior of HNACs was similar in low and high-pressure regions. Among HNAC nanoadsorbents, the HNAC-812 and 802 samples possessed the highest H2S (9.28 and 8.33 mmol g−1) and CO2 (4.28 and 4.54 mmol g−1) adsorption capacity at 1 bar and H2S (18.0 and 19.64 mmol g−1) and CO2 (6.2 and 8.33 mmol g−1) adsorption capacity at 10 bar and 25 °C, respectively. Based on the presented results, it can be inferred that the mesopores were highly effective in adsorption at high pressures. In contrast, there is more complicated description for the adsorption at the ambient pressure. As discussed in previous works [3,9,59], micropores possessing the size of below 1 nm diameter, are the dominant sites and pores which proceed the gas adsorption at atmospheric pressure; however, in this work the distinct relation was found between the micropores and the adsorption capacity. In addition, it was clarified that some factors rather than only the textural properties are influential in the adsorption process. For example, the functional groups which are formed through in-situ doping or functionalizing are indeed effective in amending the adsorption process of the polar molecules such as H2S or CO2 through improving the interaction energies [47,60]. So, it is imperative to elucidate the impact of both factors, i.e. micropores volume and functional groups, on the performance of the prepared nanocarbonaceous adsorbents at a low pressure. The HNAC-812 and 802 samples which had the highest micropore volume with about 2.63 and 2.23 at% N content among the prepared HNACs, exhibited the greatest H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity at 1 bar and 25 °C. However, increasing the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio and activation time led to a decrease in the micropores volume and an increase in the mesopores volume, eventually resulting in a reduction of the surface area and absorption capacity of H2S and CO2. Apparently, the gas adsorption behavior on HNAC with an increase in the KOH mass ratio up to 4 and 3 for H2S and CO2, respectively, was almost linear, from which, the lowest adsorption capacities are represented, indicating a significant decrease in the surface area and volume of micro and mesopores. Developing the KOH mass ratio to 4, resulted in the rate of CO2 gas absorption at both 1 and 10 bar pressures to be practically negligible. It is noteworthy that textural properties of nanoadsorbants which were synthesized at the activation temperature of 900 °C, exhibited similar behavior to that prepared with KOH mass ratio of 4. The H2S and CO2 gas uptake of HNAC samples at 25 °C are summarized in Table S3. However, the adsorption capacity of HNAC nanoadsorbants synthesized in this work, was competitive with the performance of other adsorbents, reported in literature, as given in Table 3, because of the strong hydrogen bonding occurring among the functional groups of the adsorbent and adsorbates.

Although in various research works, the CO2 adsorption isotherms have been focused on and examined in detail, especially by carbonaceous adsorbents, negligible works have been devoted to assessing the H2S adsorption isotherms. Hence, to compare the H2S adsorption capacity of the prepared nanoporous carbons with commercial sorbents, the H2S adsorption isotherms were measured for a variety of counterparts (Table 4 ). Among diverse commercial adsorbents, the 13X and 4A molecular sieves (i.e. the most common materials for removing H2S) possessed the greatest H2S removal performance with the adsorption capacity being 5.27 and 2.82 mmol g−1, respectively, while HNAC-812 and 802 exhibited 18.00 and 19.65 mmol g−1, which were about 3.7 and 7 times higher than the values obtained for the 13X and 4A adsorbents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of experimental factors on adsorption capacity of HNACs
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical and textural characteristics of porous nanoadsorbent are directly related to the structure and properties of the starting raw materials, which can be tuned according to the conditions chosen in the activation process [3,65]. Generally, the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio, the activation temperature and time are the most significant factors influencing the specific surface area, pore volume and pore size distribution of the prepared HNACs. Therefore, the effect of optimum conditions on the H2S and CO2 uptake of the HNACs at 1–10 bar was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8c-h, various factors can considerably affect the adsorption capacity. The H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity indicates that micropores are formed properly through reducing the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio, activation time and increasing the activation temperature, leading to a higher gas adsorption at low pressure. However, reversing the first two of the above-mentioned factors could contribute to formation of mesopores, leading to higher gas adsorption at a high pressure.

Fig. 8c and 8f illustrates that as the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio increased, the H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity decreased rapidly at both high and low pressures. By increasing the activating agent, the reaction between KOH and the carbon microstructure was intensified and accordingly new pores were induced and ablation of the carbon structure occurred. Along with the enlargement of the pore size, decline in the specific surface area and also adsorptive performance was observed.

According to Fig. 8d and 8 g, the H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity of HNAC-x12 synthesized at 600–700 °C is relatively similar, which was then increased by rising temperature from 700 °C to 800 °C, but as the temperature was further increased, the specific surface area and gas adsorption capacity diminished quickly. (As previously detailed in Sections 3.5. and 3.7.)

Fig. 8e and 8 h demonstrates the influence of activation time on the H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity of HNAC-8y2 nanoadsorbents. It is observable that along with an increment of the activation duration, the H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity was slightly reduced, first up to 1 h, which can be alluded to the occurrence of the chemisorption and alteration in the porosity structure via the thermal decomposition of nitrogen containing functional groups at high temperatures. By prolonging the activation time to 2 h, the carbon atoms existing over the carbon network were consumed which resulted in increasing the micropores width and also creates mesopores or macropores, finally inducing a significant decrement in the adsorption capacity.

Based on the above results, the KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio of 2 was followed at the activation temperature of 800 °C and activation time of 0–1 h as the best optimal conditions for H2S and CO2 adsorption at 1–10 bar. Hence, the HNAC-812 and 802 were the optimized samples in the present study.

Actually, it is essential to investigate the adsorbents performance in operational conditions, i.e. in the pressure range of 1–10 bar. Therefore, the H2S and CO2 adsorption isotherms, obtained via experiments, were fitted on three common mathematical models, (i.e., Langmuir (L, Equation (10)), Freundlich (F, Equation (11)) and Langmuir-Freundlich (LF, Equation (12)) [66]:

where q displays the equilibrium adsorption capacity (mmol g−1) and qm indicates the maximum adsorption capacity at the saturation state (mmol g−1); P denotes the equilibrium gas pressure (bar); Kf is the Freundlich constant; n is the deviation from the ideal homogeneous surface; and b is the affinity factor (1/bar).

In order to fit the experimental data on different models including Langmuir, Freundlich and Langmuir-Freundlich, the Curve Fitting Toolbox of MATLAB was utilized. The calculated fitting coefficients of the mentioned models for both H2S and CO2 adsorption on all the synthesized HNAC samples along with their corresponding regression coefficients (R2) are listed in Table S4 and S5. According to the regression coefficients (R2), which is a meaningful measure to determine the fitting degree of the results obtained from the adsorption experiments, L and LF models demonstrated the least and the highest accuracies, respectively, in describing the adsorption isotherms of hydrogen sulfide and carbon dioxide on all the presented HNACs. The LF model was properly fitted on all adsorption isotherms, with the regression coefficient being higher than the 0.99. The Langmuir model illustrated the existence of a monolayer cover of the gas atop the adsorbent surface and stipulates that the adsorption can be done at specific homogeneous sites. Considering the multilayer adsorption over a heterogeneous surface with a random adsorption energy distribution, actually the “amorphous” surface, i.e. the Freundlich model is valid [67]. The LF binding model is well suited in modeling both homogeneous and heterogeneous binding surfaces. Hence, the above results suggest that the multilayer adsorption would take place onto the prepared nanocarbonaceous samples, where, the energy distribution on the heterogeneous surfaces is almost non-uniform.

The regeneration efficiency and reversibility of the H2S and CO2 adsorption onto the novel HANC nanosorbents was evaluated and examined through performing ten consecutive adsorption-desorption experiments. After each cycle, the used samples were regenerated by a pressure-temperature swing method at 120 °C for 4 h under vacuum pressure. To this end, the spent sorbents were cooled down to the room temperature slowly. Then, H2S and/or CO2 gas adsorption was carried out on the regenerated adsorbents. The consecutive adsorption-desorption experiments were performed on HNAC-812 nanoadsorbent for ten times and the resulted isotherms, at 1–10 bar and 25 °C, are plotted in Fig. S8. The regeneration efficiency for each cycle was calculated by equation (13)

where the regeneration efficiency corresponding to different cycles (i) is shown by Rfi, the adsorption capacity at the initial cycles is given by q1,j in which j denotes the pressure (j = 1, 2, 3,…, 10 bar), and qi j is the adsorption capacity at the ith cycle and jth pressure. The average regeneration efficiency for H2S and CO2 are reported in Fig. 9 . As it is demonstrated, the H2S and CO2 adsorption capacity of HNAC-812 sample maintained nearly 94.06% and 96.90% of its initial value, presenting a significant adsorption efficiency; implying that it can be considered for cyclic adsorption operations in industrial scale. Moreover, the highest decline in the adsorption capacity, for both gases, occurred at the initial cycle which reveals that the micropores were significantly influenced after the first adsorption experiment . In addition, considering the fact that the H2S molecules lead to corrosion and also react with the active adsorptive sites, an almost weaker cyclic performance was achieved compared to CO2. Hence, the HNAC nanoadsorbents not only can be employed as a promising adsorbent with great reversibility for long-term adsorption-desorption operations, but also has a great stability against H2S and CO2 gases.

Highly nitrogen doped nanoporous carbon with a nitrogen content of up to 2.63 at% and an extra-high H2S and CO2 uptake was studied through a simple chemical activation of N-CQDs as a nitrogen-rich carbon precursor. Herein, for the first time, the amorphous and spherical structure of N-CQDs was destroyed and large graphene sheets with a dense porosity feature was formed. Synthetic parameters such as KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio, activation temperature and activation time were altered to maximize the H2S and CO2 adsorption at different pressures. Considering the optimal conditions, the samples prepared with a KOH/N-CQDs mass ratio of 2 for 0–1 h at 800 °C exhibited the highest gas uptake among all synthesized adsorbents. The optimized HNAC-812 and 802 samples demonstrated an excellent surface area (1477 and 1544 m2 g−1) and pore volume (1.05 and 0.95 cm3 g−1) respectively, as well as abundance of functional groups including 2.63 and 2.23 at% N and 22.42 and 27.52 at% O. In addition, the type-I isotherms were detected along with an unprecedented H2S and CO2 adsorption at both atmospheric and high pressures, where the capturing capacity was substantially better than the carbonaceous and commercial sorbents. Moreover, the gas reversibility of the HNAC samples was substantial with a significant durability even after the 10th cycle. The best performance in the sample possessing lowest surface area and pore volume (i.e. HNAC-824), deciphered that the H2S gas adsorption capacity is mainly originated from the functional groups and defects induced by nitrogen doping. The excellent gas uptake and recyclability of HNAC-812 and 802 can be potentially useful for desulfurization and carbon dioxide capture from natural gas and biogas.
